elements.content: Interview Transcript

elements.speaker: UNKNOWN MALE
elements.content: For the record, state your full name, current occupation, and current posting.

elements.speaker: MCQUAIL
elements.content: Corporal Benjamin "Benny" McQuail, Const Tech, 4ESR at 5th Canadian Division Support Base Gagetown, New Brunswick.

elements.speaker: UNKNOWN MALE
elements.content:
Construction Technician.

Interesting choice.

Why construction engineering instead of combat engineering, electrical engineering, or mechanical systems?

And before you answer, understand that I am not asking what the recruiting office answer is.

I'm asking what your answer is.

elements.speaker: MCQUAIL
elements.content:
Simple math, honestly.

If the base fails, everything inside it goes down with it.

You can have the best vehicles and the sharpest tactics, but without a functional platform to launch from, you're dead in the water.

I wanted to protect the foundation.

elements.speaker: UNKNOWN MALE
elements.content:
You said foundation.

Interesting choice of wording.

Most people say unit.

Or base.

Or country.

You said foundation.

Why?

elements.speaker: MCQUAIL
elements.content:
Because a base is more than the buildings.

It's the power, the roads, the water, the comms, the storage, the workshops, the boring systems nobody thinks about until they stop working.

I don't really see that as separate from the mission.

People talk about protecting the country, protecting the unit, protecting the people beside them, and that's fine. But none of that works if the ground underneath them gives out.

So yeah, I said foundation.

Not as a slogan or anything.

That's just what it is.

If you give me the blueprints to that foundation, I'll keep it standing.

It's what I do.

elements.speaker: UNKNOWN MALE
elements.content:
Suppose the structure you're supporting extends beyond a single base.

Beyond a single province.

Beyond a single country.

Suppose millions of people rely on systems they will never see, maintained by people they will never meet.

Would your answer change?

elements.speaker: MCQUAIL
elements.content: No.

elements.speaker: UNKNOWN MALE
elements.content:
Suppose the machinery keeping the world upright was not merely complicated.

Suppose it was impossible.

Suppose some of it actively resisted understanding.

Suppose some parts wanted to be found.

Suppose some parts wanted to find you.

Would you still volunteer to maintain it?

Or would there be a point where you decide the foundation should collapse?

elements.speaker: MCQUAIL
elements.content:
You're asking if I have a breaking point.

Everyone does.

If a system is actively malicious; if it's designed to destroy the people feeding it, then it's not a foundation anymore; it's a trap.

But if it's just hostile, stubborn, and weird?

That's just a bad shift on an old deployment.

If millions of people are relying on it to keep breathing, you don't pull the plug just because the machinery makes you uncomfortable.

You build a bigger cage around it, you double the dampeners, and you keep it running.

elements.speaker: MCQUAIL
elements.content: What was that?

elements.speaker: UNKNOWN MALE
elements.content: Equipment issue.

elements.speaker: UNKNOWN MALE
elements.content:
Suppose the cage fails.

Suppose one day you arrive at work and discover that the dampeners failed six hours ago.

Suppose containment failed four hours ago.

Suppose everyone who could fix the problem is already dead.

What do you do?

You do not have reinforcements.

You do not have support.

You do not have orders.

Communication is unavailable.

Extraction is unavailable.

You are not expected to survive.

What do you do, Corporal McQuail?
Audio artifact present between timestamps ██:██:██ and ██:██:██. The following statement was not acknowledged by either participant during the interview.

elements.speaker: MCQUAIL
elements.content:
If I'm the last one standing, then the mission isn't "survive."

The mission is mitigation.

I look for the fail-safes.

Every engineer builds a kill-switch into a dangerous system. Something meant to burn the house down before the fire spreads to the neighborhood.

If communication is dead and extraction is gone, my only job left is to pull that switch.

I'll fight my way to the control console, lock the blast doors from the inside, and trigger the scuttle sequence.

If I'm not expected to survive, I might as well make sure the problem doesn't survive either.

elements.speaker: UNKNOWN MALE
elements.content:
Most people answer that question incorrectly.

Most people say they'd run.

Some say they'd fight.

Some say they'd save themselves so they could warn others.

You looked for the containment procedures.

Interesting.

elements.speaker: UNKNOWN MALE
elements.content:
Last question.

This one is important.

Suppose you discover that the people giving the orders are wrong.

Not malicious.

Not corrupt.

Wrong.

Suppose procedure says one thing.

Reality says another.

Suppose following orders gets people killed.

Suppose disobeying orders gets you court martialed.

Who carries the responsibility?

The person who wrote the order?

Or the person who followed it?

elements.speaker: MCQUAIL
elements.content:
You're trying to see if I'm a rogue or a drone.

I'm neither.

If an order is wrong because the leadership doesn't have the data on the ground, then it's my job to feed them the data.

But if there's no time, no comms, and people are dying?

The responsibility falls on me.

I fix the problem first, and I argue with the lawyers later.

If you want a man who will blindly walk off a cliff because a piece of paper told him to, you brought the wrong Corporal into this room.

I maintain the foundation.

I don't let it collapse just to keep the paperwork clean.
SUPPLEMENTAL OBSERVER NOTES
Compiled from witness testimony and post-interview reconstruction. Original audio recording concluded prior to the following exchange.
Subject remained seated for approximately four seconds following recorder deactivation.
Interviewer stated that the subject's final response was not considered part of the formal interview.
Subject requested identification of interviewing personnel. Interviewer declined to provide organizational affiliation.
Interviewer placed an unmarked contact card on the table and instructed subject to retain it.
Subject appeared confused but did not refuse the card.
Interviewer advised subject that he would eventually be offered a choice, then exited the room.
POST-INTERVIEW EVALUATION
Subject displays strong containment-oriented thinking and an unusually high tolerance for ambiguity.
Subject repeatedly frames institutions, infrastructure, and societal systems as a singular "foundation" despite lacking contextual exposure to Foundation terminology.
Subject demonstrates independent judgment while maintaining strong procedural adherence.
Risk factors include elevated likelihood of future administrative conflict and willingness to deviate from standing orders during perceived emergency situations.
Recommendation: APPROVED FOR RECRUITMENT. Recommended assignment: Site-65 Technical Services Division. Continue observation.
